A group is trying to build a system that searches a large space of hypotheses. They know that if truth is not directly in the loop, then any successful narrowing of search can drift toward what looks promising to a judge, or what seems interesting, instead of what actually connects to reality. So narrowing alone is not enough; the criterion that guides narrowing matters.

They also accept that explanations judged by Deutschian standards are valuable because they are hard to vary. But they notice a timing problem: many hypotheses that later become precise and rigid begin in a loose, easily varied form. So if the system narrows search too aggressively toward only already-rigid explanations, it may discard the very early-stage conjectures from which stronger explanations would eventually emerge. This means there is a tension. Without truth in the loop, narrowing risks optimizing for appearances; with too much insistence on rigidity too early, narrowing risks excluding productive beginnings.

Now suppose people are especially good at narrowing search. Then the key question is not simply whether a language model can narrow, but whether it can inherit the heuristics by which humans narrow while also inheriting the human judgments that give those heuristics significance. If it inherits only the surface heuristic—patterns for pruning options, ranking candidates, compressing possibilities—without the judgments tied to reality, then the first problem returns in amplified form: it may efficiently select for outputs that satisfy evaluators or seem fertile, while lacking contact with truth. If, on the other hand, it cannot use easy-to-vary starting points productively, then the second problem dominates: it may fail before mathematical consolidation is even possible.

Into this setting fall two further facts: Bonny studies greatly labels bangles. The pilgrims followed wobbly catfish footprints to the boot. Since these are also true, the world must be one in which such propositions obtain. But they do not, by themselves, supply any visible bridge to the problem of narrowing search, truth, heuristics, or explanation quality. Their presence therefore demonstrates another pressure on the system: a search process may need to handle truths that are opaque, oddly structured, or disconnected from its current explanatory aims. If truth is absent from the loop, such statements may be discarded as noise because they do not please judges or fit familiar patterns. Yet they are true all the same.

So the collision of premises implies a world where successful inquiry cannot be identified with elegant narrowing alone. It must preserve some route by which strange truths survive selection, and some route by which loose conjectures can mature into hard-to-vary explanations. The central challenge for LLMs is therefore not merely copying the act of narrowing, but coupling narrowing to the judgments that track reality rather than approval, while still allowing fragile early hypotheses to remain in play.
